titleStmt.title: Transcription and Translation of NRS GD55/266

respStmt.resp: Exported from Models of Authority data
respStmt.name: tei_exporter

availability.p: Status unknown. Exported for research and migration workflows.

revisionDesc.change: transcription moa=353 status=live modified=2021-04-06 12:40:01.432243+00 | translation moa=354 status=live modified=2021-04-06 12:40:03.063835+00

div.p:
. A . dei Gratia Rex scott’ . Omnibus probis hominibus tocius terre sue Clericis et laicis salutem . Sciant presentes et futuri nos ad honorem
dei et gloriose uirginis matris sue . necnon et pro salute nostra et antecessorum et successorum nostrorum . dedisse concessisse . et hac carta nostra confirmasse
monachis de Melros’ deo ibidem seruientibus et inperpetuum seruituris piscariam illam super Twed’ . que uocatur piscaria de Selechirk’ . Et septem
acras terre cum edificiis et prato infra easdem septem acras terre contentis . sicut eedem septem acre terre per fossatum et sepem eorundem monachorum die
huius collationis includebantur . Et eciam pasturam ad Octo boues . et Octo uaccas in Wauhop’ . Concessimus etiam dictis monachis vt ipsi per uisum foresta
riorum nostrorum qui pro tempore erunt . habeant et capiant in foresto nostro maremium ad sustentationem stagni sui in eadem piscaria . Tenend’ . et habend’ .
eisdem inperpetuum in liberam . puram . et perpetuam elemosinam . ita libere quiete . plenarie . et honorifice : sicut aliqua elemosina ex dono nostro ab aliquibus uiris
religiosis in regno nostro . liberius . quiecius . plenius et honorificentius tenetur uel possidetur . Testibus . venerabili patre W episcopo Glasguensi . Cancellario nostro . Philippo . abbate
de Ieddewurd’ . Dauid de Lindes’ . Iustic’ . Laodonie . Bernardo de Rippeley . Apud Ieddewurd’ . Octauo die Februarii . anno regni . domini regis . Tricesimo tercio .

Alexander by the grace of God king of the Scots sends greeting to all worthy men, clerk and lay, of the whole of his land. Let present and future know us to have given, granted, and by this our charter made firm, for the honour of God and his glorious virgin Mother, as well as for our own salvation and our ancestors’ and successors’, to the monks of Melrose serving God in that place and who will serve there in perpetuity, the fishery on the Tweed which is called the fishery of Selkirk; and seven acres of land with buildings and a meadow contained within those seven acres of land, just as the same seven acres of land of the same monks were enclosed by a ditch and fence on the day of this conveyance; and also a pasture for eight oxen and eight cows in ‘Wauhop’. We have also allowed to the said monks that they, by the view of our foresters at the time, may have and take timber in our forest for the upkeep of their stank in the same fishery. For them to hold and to have for ever in free, pure, and everlasting alms, as freely, quitely, fully, and honourably as any alms out of our gift is freely, quitely, fully and honourably held or possessed by any religious men in our kingdom. As witnesses, the venerable father William, bishop of Glasgow, our chancellor; Philip, abbot of Jedburgh; David of Lindsay, justiciar of Lothian; Bernard of Ripley. At Jedburgh on the eighth day of February, in the thirty-third year of the reign of the lord king [1247].
